tldr: Moving from LÖVE (a minimal Lua 2D framework) to Unreal Engine is a full platform shift, not a port. Plan to rebuild assets, scripting, and runtime from scratch around Unreal's editor and Blueprint/C++ workflow.
why_switch: Want a visual editor and asset pipeline
Target consoles or mobile with first-party tooling
Tap MetaHuman, Niagara, and built-in physics
Hire from a larger Unreal talent pool
what_to_export: Lua game logic and state machines
export_method: LÖVE projects are plain folders of Lua scripts, images, audio, and fonts (often shipped as a .love zip). There is no platform export step — copy the project directory and treat each subfolder as a candidate for migration. Document your scene structure, entity classes, and update/draw loop on paper before touching Unreal, because LÖVE's immediate-mode draw model has no direct Unreal counterpart. Catalog assets by type: PNG/JPG sprites, OGG/WAV audio, TTF fonts, and any custom binary data. Note any C modules or LuaJIT FFI bindings — these will not carry over and must be reimplemented in C++ or Blueprint. If you use love.physics (Box2D), record body/fixture configs since Unreal uses Chaos physics with different parameter semantics.
import_method: Install Unreal Engine via the Epic Games Launcher and create a new project (Blank or 2D template if available in your version). Import sprites and textures through the Content Browser drag-and-drop; configure as Texture2D with appropriate compression. Audio imports as Sound Wave assets. Recreate levels as Unreal Levels with Actors and Components — there is no automatic tilemap importer from LÖVE. Reimplement game logic in Blueprints (visual scripting) or C++; Lua does not run natively. For 2D-style games, use Paper2D or similar. Set up input through the Enhanced Input system. Test packaging via Platforms menu for your target. Plan iterative milestones rather than a big-bang port.
effort: 4-12 weeks for a small game; multiple months for larger projects
gotchas: No Lua runtime in Unreal by default — third-party plugins exist but are not first-class.
LÖVE's immediate-mode 2D drawing has no direct equivalent; expect to redesign rendering logic.
Asset compression and texture settings differ; sprites may look different until tuned.
Unreal's royalty model (5% past a per-product threshold) differs from LÖVE's zlib license — review terms.
Build sizes and hardware requirements jump significantly compared to LÖVE binaries.
Hot-reload workflow in Unreal is not as fast as editing Lua and pressing run.

email.subject: Planning a 14-day move from LÖVE to Unreal Engine
email.body:
Hi team,

We're planning a migration from LÖVE to Unreal Engine over the next 14 days. The goal is to lift our prototype onto an engine with a proper editor, broader platform support, and richer rendering tools.

Proposed timeline:
Days 1-3: Audit the LÖVE project — list all assets, scripts, scenes, and external dependencies. Decide what carries over and what gets rebuilt.
Days 4-7: Stand up a new Unreal project, import textures and audio, and rebuild the core gameplay loop in Blueprints or C++.
Days 8-11: Recreate levels, input bindings, and audio cues. Wire up save/load and localization.
Days 12-13: QA pass on target platforms, fix performance and packaging issues.
Day 14: Sign-off review and tag the milestone build.

We expect rough edges — Lua logic does not transfer automatically and 2D rendering will need redesign. Please flag any LÖVE-specific tricks (FFI, custom shaders, love.physics tuning) early so we can plan replacements.

Thanks,
Your customer
